skill.name: azure-ai-voicelive-ts
skill.description: Guidance for building JavaScript and TypeScript apps with the Azure AI Voice Live SDK, including authentication, sessions, audio streaming, events, voices, and browser usage.
skill.summary: Build real-time voice AI apps with Azure AI Voice Live in JavaScript and TypeScript.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, environment access, weak cryptography, and a critical combination heuristic. Review found these are documentation examples, placeholder Azure endpoints, reference links, and Markdown inline code rather than executable skill logic. The only confirmed concern is a low-risk API key constructor example that may encourage unsafe copy-paste if users do not follow the later credential guidance.

low_findings.title: API Key Constructor Example May Encourage Copy-Paste Secrets
low_findings.description: The API key authentication example uses a literal placeholder credential in AzureKeyCredential. It is not a real secret and the skill later recommends DefaultAzureCredential, but users could copy the pattern into source code instead of loading secrets securely.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential value is clearly a placeholder, so this is not credential leakage. The pattern is still a plausible documentation safety issue because it demonstrates an inline API key constructor.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The reported shell and Ruby backtick findings occur in Markdown code fences, TypeScript examples, tables, and inline code. No standalone script, hook, or executable skill file was found in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are visibly Markdown documentation blocks or tables. There is no evidence of Ruby execution, shell interpolation, or runtime command invocation in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Network and Environment Findings Are Expected SDK Documentation
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are placeholder Azure endpoints or official package and documentation links. Environment variable references document expected Azure configuration and do not show exfiltration or hidden credential collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to Azure, npm, GitHub, and Microsoft Learn resources, and the environment variables are named for Azure Voice Live configuration. No evidence found of unauthorized network destinations or secret exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts correspond to ordinary documentation text and table content, not calls to MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other cryptographic APIs. No cryptographic implementation was found in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations contain descriptions, model names, and schema text. I did not find evidence of actual cryptographic algorithms or security-sensitive hashing code.

content.user_title: Build Azure Voice Live TypeScript Apps
content.value_statement: Real-time voice AI apps require careful setup for authentication, sessions, audio formats, and event handling. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Azure Voice Live guidance for JavaScript and TypeScript projects.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to create VoiceLiveClient and start VoiceLiveSession connections.
Documents session configuration for audio, text, voices, turn detection, and tools.
Maps Voice Live event handlers for text, audio, transcription, errors, and function calls.
Compares supported voices, models, turn detection modes, and audio formats.
Includes browser setup notes for InteractiveBrowserCredential and microphone access.
limitations: It is documentation guidance, not a complete runnable application.
It does not provide full microphone capture or audio playback implementations.
It relies on Azure service availability, credentials, and SDK behavior outside the skill.
It references beta SDK behavior that may change in future Azure releases.

use_cases.title: Prototype a voice assistant
use_cases.description: Use the quick start and session examples to create a TypeScript voice assistant with real-time audio and text responses.

use_cases.title: Plan production authentication
use_cases.description: Compare DefaultAzureCredential, API key usage, endpoint variables, and browser credentials before integrating Azure Voice Live.

use_cases.title: Implement event handling
use_cases.description: Use the event reference to handle connection lifecycle, transcription, response streaming, function calls, and cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Start a basic project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up a minimal TypeScript project that uses Azure AI Voice Live with DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Configure a session
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to configure an Azure Voice Live session for audio and text with a standard Azure voice and server VAD.

prompt_templates.title: Handle streaming events
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through handling VoiceLiveSession events for transcript deltas, audio deltas, errors, and connection cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Design function calling
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a Voice Live function-calling flow that validates arguments, returns tool output, and resumes response generation.

output_examples.input: I need a plan for adding Azure Voice Live to a Node.js assistant.
output: A setup checklist covering package installation, Azure credentials, endpoint configuration, and model selection.
A session configuration plan for audio, text, voice choice, turn detection, and cleanup.
Implementation notes for streaming transcript and audio events.

output_examples.input: I am seeing connection and authentication errors in my Voice Live prototype.
output: A troubleshooting path that separates endpoint, credential, network, and protocol failures.
Recommended logging settings and event handlers to capture useful diagnostics.
Safer credential handling guidance that avoids embedding API keys in source files.

output_examples.input: I want browser microphone input with Azure Voice Live.
output: A browser integration outline using interactive authentication, microphone permission, and audio context setup.
Notes about bundler requirements and client-side security boundaries.
Reminders to handle permission denial, cleanup, and supported audio formats.

best_practices: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential or managed identity for server applications whenever possible.
Validate function-call arguments before calling external services or returning tool output.
Close subscriptions and sessions when audio interaction ends.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode Azure API keys, tenant IDs, or client IDs in committed source files.
Do not assume browser microphone access is always granted or available.
Do not ignore streaming errors, disconnect events, or protocol failures.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build JavaScript and TypeScript applications that use Azure AI Voice Live for real-time voice interactions.

faq.question: Does it include a complete sample app?
faq.answer: No. It provides focused SDK guidance and examples, but not a complete application with full audio plumbing.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is structured for Claude, Claude Code, and Codex workflows.

faq.question: Which authentication method is preferred?
faq.answer: DefaultAzureCredential is preferred for most server-side Azure workloads because it avoids hardcoded secrets.

faq.question: Does it cover browser usage?
faq.answer: Yes. It notes browser credentials, bundler requirements, microphone access, and audio context setup.

faq.question: Why is the security risk low?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation-only. Static alerts were mostly Markdown examples, with one low-risk API key copy-paste concern.
